glossary.definition: a Guide to Intelligent Systems.
glossary.sentence: Artificial Intelligence: a Guide to Intelligent Systems. Addison Wesley. 540 p.

glossary.definition: A game where the initial state is a blank grid, players make moves by putting marks in empty squares to reach goal states consisting of three marks in a horizontal, vertical or diagonal row.
glossary.sentence: In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid, which appears when you start thelagio game (Figure 4). Each player wants toreche reach one of the goal states, in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row (Figure 4). To reach a goal state, thes players make moves by putting their marks in empty squares.

glossary.definition: A graph that can be complete or partial, used to represent the game and its results.
glossary.sentence: Step 25: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph...

glossary.definition: The algorithm reduces the consumption of computational resources (search time and computer memory), which is the main problem with Breadth-First Search.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm reduces theContesmation of computational resources (search time and respects the algorithm. The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions. The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21). Breadth-Fist Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches. Bidirectional Search starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node. It explores closer branches of both nodes until it finds a point of contact. The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. Thealgorithmuses two OPEN lists and two/or two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions. Bidirectional Search includes an additional operational step (Figure glossary.definition: Performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the value of β is smaller than α.

glossary.definition: A measure assigned by the algorithm when it is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure, such as when a node is at the MAX level and only one known heuristic measure is known (>=1 or >=-1), or when a node is at the MIN level and only one known heuristic and no other unconsidered descendants are present.
glossary.sentence: Step 14: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure...

glossary.definition: A measure acquired by a node based on its descendants' measures, which can be assigned when there are no other unconsidered descendants or when it is at the MAX level and acquires the maximum from direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: Step 15: After making the cut off, the node... acquires a precise heuristic measure...

glossary.definition: An entity whose task is to win the game and who seeks higher measures in the best case.
glossary.sentence: Step 17: ...since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: An entity whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.
glossary.sentence: Step 22: ...since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser...

glossary.definition: An algorithm used to provide game results, which can be applied to a complete state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Step 25: ...This is a game result, and we can as see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A technique used to exclude nodes from evaluation and cut off branches in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Step 25: ...A more interesting issue is the efficiency of alpha-beta pruning.

glossary.definition: A more complex case where the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: Step 25: A partial state space graph is a more complex case...

glossary.definition: An approach used to process a partial state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Step 25: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to be process a partial state space graph...

glossary.definition: An approach used to process a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that requires defining thevalue of hyperparameter k, which represents the chosen number of neighbours that will be examined. For each data object in the dataset, it calculates the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object and the current data object from the dataset; stores the index and distances in a list; sorts the list in ascending order; picks up the top k data objects; gets labels; identifies output for the new data object: returns the mode of k labels for classification task and the mean of k labels for regression task. It typically demands normalization of feature value, is simple enough with few hyperparameters to tune, has no separate training phase, uses all data in the dataset for predictions, and does not have assumptions about data/data distribution. However, it can be be computationally expensive and time-consuming with a large number of data objects because it calculates distances between all training data objects and a new one.
glossary.sentence: kNN algorithm typically demands the normalization of feature value.

glossary.definition: a function for combining input signals (for example, the summation function )
glossary.sentence:
Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an 
output that is được sent to other neurons again. ... A typical artificial neural network comprises a hierarchy of layers, A typical artificial neural network comprises a hierarchy of It's a function for combining input signals (for example, the summation function )

glossary.definition: a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base; triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed; control actions for slot-level manipulations; usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames; object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames; can have an executable part (the/code describing what will happen when the demon is called) developed by the developer of the intelligent computer system; can be added to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity; common demons: IF-NEEDED (triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it), IF-CHANGED (triggered when a slot value changes), IF-ADDED (triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame), IF-REMOVED (triggered when a slot value is deleted)
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a                erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_ererased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_erased_er and no knowledge of the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A system with five core components: knowledge base, database or working memory, inference engine, explanation facilities, and user interface. It can also include additional components like external applications and developer interface. The knowledge base includes IF... rules. The system must keep track of the current state of the problem-solving process (case-specific data) and the working memory ensures this by storing facts, conclusions, confidence measures, information from external programs, dead ends, and other relevant information. It can perform two types of search: data-driven search (forward chaining) and goal-driven search (backward chaining).
